title: Berlin Conference begins the Scramble for Africa
summary: On 15 November 1884 the representatives of fourteen states gathered in the palace of the German chancellor Otto von Bismarck at Wilhelmstrasse 77 in Berlin to regulate the European partition of Africa. Over the following three months they set the rules of the Scramble that was already under way, recognised the personal sovereignty of King Leopold II of the Belgians over a vast tract of the Congo basin, and imposed on all future acquisitions the principle of effective occupation. Not a single African was present. The General Act signed on 26 February 1885 accelerated a conquest that within thirty years would leave only Ethiopia and Liberia formally independent.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
European interest in Africa had accelerated dramatically in the fifteen years before the conference. The opening of the Suez Canal in November 1869 had given Britain a strategic reason to secure the whole Nile valley; the collapse of Egyptian solvency in 1876 and the British occupation of Egypt after the battle of Tel-el-Kebir on 13 September 1882 turned this abstract interest into a formal presence. The French, expelled from Egypt by the British, compensated themselves in West Africa and the Sahara, pushing the flag from Senegal into the interior under governors such as Louis Faidherbe. The Portuguese, who had held coastal enclaves in Angola and Mozambique since the sixteenth century, tried to consolidate them into a transcontinental empire. In southern Africa the annexation of the Transvaal in 1877, the Anglo-Zulu War of 1879 and the first Anglo-Boer War of 1880 to 1881 signalled the acceleration of European involvement.

The personal catalyst was King Leopold II of the Belgians, sovereign of a small constitutional monarchy who had never reconciled himself to Belgium's status as a minor state. From 1876 Leopold had used a series of philanthropic and geographical societies, most notably the Association Internationale Africaine and its successor the Association Internationale du Congo, to acquire in his own name treaties with African rulers along the Congo River. His agent was Henry Morton Stanley, the Welsh-American journalist whose Anglo-American expedition of 1874 to 1877 had descended the Congo from Lake Tanganyika to the Atlantic. In parallel the French naval officer Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza, on behalf of the French government, was signing his own treaties on the north bank of the river with the Bateke king Makoko. In 1884 both projects reached crisis. Britain, alarmed by French movements in the Congo estuary, recognised Portuguese sovereignty over the coast in a treaty of 26 February 1884; France and Germany objected. Portuguese, French, Belgian, British and German claims threatened to collide in central Africa.

The German chancellor Otto von Bismarck had been slow to enter the African competition. His famous remark that his map of Africa lay in Europe reflected a lifelong preference for European over colonial politics. In April 1884, however, the German merchant Adolf Luderitz raised the German flag over Angra Pequena on the Namibian coast; in July Gustav Nachtigal secured for Germany the strip that became Togo and the Cameroons; in the same summer Karl Peters was busy signing land-grabbing treaties in East Africa. To manage these rivalries and to embarrass Britain, Bismarck accepted a French proposal from Prime Minister Jules Ferry that Berlin should host an international conference on West African affairs. Invitations went out on 15 October 1884; the conference opened a month later. Fourteen powers were represented: the German Empire, the Austro-Hungarian Empire, Belgium, Denmark, Spain, the United States, France, the United Kingdom, Italy, the Netherlands, Portugal, Russia, Sweden-Norway and the Ottoman Empire. The single non-European power, the United States, sent the historian John Kasson as its plenipotentiary. Leopold's International Congo Association had no formal seat but was represented behind the scenes and would be the great winner of the meeting.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The delegates convened at three in the afternoon of Saturday 15 November 1884 in the great hall of the Reichskanzlerpalais on Wilhelmstrasse. Bismarck welcomed them with a short speech setting out three tasks: to guarantee free trade in the Congo basin; to guarantee free navigation on the Congo and the Niger; and to agree on the formalities to be observed for future annexations of African coastline. Notably, none of these tasks involved consulting Africans themselves. The presiding officer was Bismarck's foreign secretary Paul von Hatzfeldt; the working committees were chaired variously by the British, French and Belgian delegates. Meetings continued through the winter, interrupted by the elaborate court entertainments of the German capital.

The first commission handled the Congo basin. It defined a Conventional Basin extending far beyond the geographical watershed to include much of central and eastern Africa. Within this basin free trade was guaranteed to all nations, no discriminatory tariffs were permitted, all religions were to be allowed to preach and all foreigners to travel and reside. The slave trade was formally condemned; the powers pledged to work for its suppression. A tacit understanding, ratified through bilateral agreements signed around the edges of the conference, recognised the sovereignty of Leopold's Association Internationale du Congo over the majority of this basin. On 22 April 1884 the United States had already recognised the Association's flag; France recognised it on 5 February 1885 in exchange for a right of pre-emption if Leopold's project failed; Germany recognised it in early November 1884 on the eve of the conference; Britain on 16 December 1884. When the conference closed on 26 February 1885 the Association was recognised by all fourteen powers. On 29 May 1885 Leopold decreed himself sovereign of what he now called the Etat Independant du Congo, the Congo Free State, a personal domain of some two and a half million square kilometres, seventy times the size of Belgium itself, in which he was to be answerable to no parliament.

The second commission handled the Niger, where British and French rivalry was more direct. Free navigation was guaranteed on the Niger and its tributaries; Britain retained a preponderant position on the lower river through the Royal Niger Company chartered in 1886.

The third commission produced the most consequential rule of the conference. Article 34 of the General Act required any power that in future 'takes possession of a tract of land on the coasts of the African continent outside of its present possessions' or that established a protectorate there to notify the other signatories, so that they might protest any competing claim. Article 35 required that any such claim be accompanied by 'a sufficient authority in the regions occupied' to enforce order and, where trade existed, to enforce respect for the treaty. This became the doctrine of effective occupation, according to which a European claim to African territory required actual administrative presence on the ground, not merely a treaty with a coastal ruler or a flag on the shore. It was intended to prevent paper empires. In practice it drove Britain, France, Germany, Portugal and Belgium to launch military expeditions inland to make good their claims. The General Act was signed on 26 February 1885 by the plenipotentiaries of all fourteen states. On the same day the conference closed. Bismarck hosted a farewell reception.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The most obvious consequence was the acceleration of the partition. In 1876 European powers had controlled roughly ten per cent of the surface of Africa, mostly along the coasts and in the far north and south; by 1914 they controlled more than ninety per cent. Only Ethiopia, which decisively defeated an Italian invasion at the battle of Adwa on 1 March 1896, and Liberia, an American-sponsored republic settled by freed African-American slaves in the 1820s, retained formal independence. France assembled a vast empire from Algiers to Brazzaville and from Dakar to Djibouti; Britain acquired a chain from Cairo to the Cape, though the intervening German East Africa spoiled Cecil Rhodes's dream of a continuous route; Germany took Togo, Cameroon, South-West Africa and East Africa; Portugal held Angola and Mozambique; Belgium had the Congo; Italy took Eritrea, Somalia and, after 1911, Libya; Spain retained small enclaves in Morocco and Rio de Oro.

The conquest was accompanied by considerable violence. The African resistance was extensive but almost always ended in defeat against European rifles, machine guns and artillery. Wars against the Zulu, the Asante, the Mahdists of Sudan, the Ndebele, Shona, Herero, Nama, Yao, Fulani, Bemba and dozens of others cost hundreds of thousands of African lives. In German South-West Africa the Herero and Nama War of 1904 to 1908 became the first genocide of the twentieth century, killing an estimated eighty per cent of the Herero and half of the Nama populations. In the Congo Free State the collection of wild rubber under a system of forced labour and mutilation, publicised from 1898 onward by Edmund Dene Morel and by the British consul Roger Casement's 1904 report, produced a death toll now estimated by historians at between eight and ten million between 1885 and 1908, when international outrage finally forced Leopold to cede the territory to the Belgian state. The Congo Reform Association of 1904 to 1913 was the first sustained mass campaign of humanitarian foreign policy of the modern age.

The economic transformation followed. Railways were built into the interior to serve mines, plantations and administrative centres: the Cape to Kimberley line was extended toward Rhodesia by Rhodes's British South Africa Company; the French built lines from Dakar to Bamako and from Djibouti to Addis Ababa; the Uganda railway ran from Mombasa to Lake Victoria; the Congo-Ocean line linked Brazzaville to the Atlantic. Cash crops such as cocoa in the Gold Coast, groundnuts in Senegal, palm oil in Nigeria, coffee in East Africa and cotton in Uganda were promoted or forced onto African cultivators. Mineral extraction, diamonds and gold in South Africa, copper in Katanga and Northern Rhodesia, tin in Nigeria, phosphates in North Africa, transformed the economic geography of the continent and drew hundreds of thousands of African workers into migrant labour.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Berlin Conference has become the standard shorthand for the European partition of Africa, so much so that the term 'scramble' entered the popular vocabulary of the years immediately after it. It did not, in strict fact, divide Africa on a map; that task was done piece by piece over the following thirty years, at bilateral negotiations in London, Paris, Berlin, Lisbon and Brussels, and by military expeditions in the field. But it set the rules of the game and it legitimised the process in international law. The straight lines drawn across the continent, especially in the Sahara and the interior of central Africa, ignored the historical, linguistic, ethnic, religious and ecological realities on the ground. The frontiers they produced were inherited almost unchanged by the independent states that emerged after 1957, and have been at the root of many of the border disputes, civil wars and refugee crises of the post-colonial era. The Organisation of African Unity in 1963 explicitly committed its members to respect existing colonial borders in the interest of continental stability; the alternative, redrawing them, was rightly feared as a source of even worse conflict.

The conference has also become the emblematic example, in modern historiography and popular memory, of the arrogance of European imperialism. The absence of any African participant, the confidence with which the delegates parcelled out a continent they had barely seen, the humanitarian rhetoric of anti-slavery and free trade that masked the coming brutality of Leopold's Congo, the technological gap between the European powers and the African societies they intended to subjugate, all combined to make Berlin a scene that would haunt the twentieth-century memory of the imperial age. Joseph Conrad's Heart of Darkness of 1899 and the writings of Frantz Fanon in the 1950s and 1960s drew on the Congolese case; the works of Adam Hochschild and other historians since the 1990s have restored the death toll of the Congo to public consciousness.

The legacy is also institutional. The Berlin Act's language on the suppression of slavery was expanded at the Brussels Anti-Slavery Conference of 1889 to 1890 and became one of the earliest examples of a multilateral humanitarian treaty regime. The idea that international conferences of the great powers could regulate whole continents anticipated the League of Nations mandate system of 1919 and, more remotely, the trusteeship provisions of the United Nations Charter of 1945. And the map of Africa itself, redrawn on the walls of the Reichskanzlerpalais in the winter of 1884 to 1885, remained, with a few small adjustments, the political map of the continent that decolonisation inherited seventy-five years later.

worldContext.text: The United States on 4 March 1885 inaugurated Grover Cleveland, the first Democratic president since the Civil War. The Statue of Liberty, gifted by France, was in its final phase of assembly on Bedloe's Island in New York Harbour; its formal dedication would follow on 28 October 1886. The country was in the middle of its great industrial expansion, with the transcontinental railroad system essentially complete and mass immigration from southern and eastern Europe beginning to reshape its cities. The Haymarket affair in Chicago on 4 May 1886 lay just ahead.

The Qing empire under the boy emperor Guangxu and the regent empress dowager Cixi was in the Sino-French War over the French protectorate in Tonkin; the war ended on 9 June 1885 with the Treaty of Tientsin recognising the French protectorate. Korea was the site of the abortive Gapsin Coup on 4 December 1884, in which pro-Japanese reformers briefly seized the palace before being expelled by Chinese troops under Yuan Shikai. In Japan the Meiji government was drafting the constitution that would be promulgated in 1889.

British India under viceroy Lord Ripon was completing the construction of its trunk railway network; the Indian National Congress would hold its first meeting in Bombay in December 1885, marking the beginning of organised Indian nationalism. In Burma, King Thibaw's dispute with a British timber company would give Britain the pretext to launch the Third Anglo-Burmese War in November 1885 and annex Upper Burma in January 1886. In Afghanistan the Panjdeh Incident of March 1885, in which Russian forces defeated an Afghan garrison, nearly brought Britain and Russia to war in the Great Game.

The Mahdist rising in Sudan reached its climax during the conference. On 26 January 1885 the Mahdists stormed Khartoum after a ten-month siege and killed the British governor-general Charles Gordon, an event that shook Britain and would eventually be avenged at Omdurman in 1898. Egypt itself was under de facto British occupation. The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Abdulhamid II continued to lose territory and to accumulate European debt; the Egyptian question and the Bulgarian crisis kept European diplomacy busy.

In Latin America, Brazil was in the last years of empire under Pedro II; the Golden Law abolishing slavery was signed on 13 May 1888 and a military coup ended the monarchy in November 1889. Argentina and Chile were negotiating the boundary in Patagonia. Cuba remained a Spanish colony; the Ten Years' War had ended in 1878 but Cuban nationalism was building toward the war of independence of 1895.

The scramble for territory was not limited to Africa. In November 1884 Germany proclaimed a protectorate over the northeastern portion of New Guinea (Kaiser-Wilhelmsland) and the Bismarck Archipelago; Britain declared its own protectorate over the southeastern portion on 6 November 1884. In the South Pacific the French and British were competing for the New Hebrides, eventually settled as a condominium in 1906. Australian colonial governments were pressing London to counter German expansion in the region.

figures.name: Otto von Bismarck
figures.role: Chancellor of the German Empire and host of the conference; used it to establish Germany as a colonial power without disturbing European alliances

figures.name: Leopold II of the Belgians
figures.role: Absent from the conference but its principal beneficiary; recognised as sovereign of what became the Congo Free State

figures.name: Henry Morton Stanley
figures.role: Welsh-American explorer and agent of Leopold II whose treaties on the Congo underpinned the Belgian claim

figures.name: Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza
figures.role: French explorer and colonial administrator who established French claims north of the Congo River

figures.name: Jules Ferry
figures.role: French prime minister and leading advocate of French colonial expansion; jointly proposed the conference with Bismarck

figures.name: John A. Kasson
figures.role: United States minister to Germany and American plenipotentiary at the conference

images.caption: Bismarck at the Berlin Conference, cutting a cake labelled Africa while other delegates look on. French satirical print in L'Illustration, 3 January 1885.
images.credit: Anonymous, L'Illustration, 3 January 1885, via Wikimedia Commons

sources.title: The General Act of the Berlin Conference on West Africa, 26 February 1885
